stem: The general solution of a second-order constant-coefficient homogeneous equation is \( y(t) = C_1 e^{[[p]]t} + C_2 e^{[[q]]t} \). Writing the equation as \( y'' + b\,y' + c\,y = 0 \), what is the value of the constant coefficient \( c \)?
solution: The exponents \( [[p]] \) and \( [[q]] \) are the characteristic roots, so the characteristic equation is \( (r - [[p]])(r - [[q]]) = r^{2} - ([[p]]+[[q]])r + [[p]][[q]] = 0 \). Matching \( y'' + b\,y' + c\,y = 0 \) gives \( c = [[p]][[q]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This gives \( b = -([[p]]+[[q]]) \), the coefficient of \( y' \), not \( c \).
This is the sum of the roots rather than their product.
This has the wrong sign; the product of the roots is \( +[[p]][[q]] \).
This subtracts the roots instead of multiplying them.
This squares one root instead of multiplying the two roots.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: The general solution of a second-order constant-coefficient homogeneous equation is \( y(t) = C_1 e^{[[p]]t} + C_2 e^{[[q]]t} \). Writing the equation as \( y'' + b\,y' + c\,y = 0 \), what is the value of the coefficient \( b \)?
solution: The exponents \( [[p]] \) and \( [[q]] \) are the characteristic roots, so the characteristic equation is \( (r-[[p]])(r-[[q]]) = r^{2} - ([[p]]+[[q]])r + [[p]][[q]] = 0 \). Matching \( y'' + b\,y' + c\,y = 0 \) gives \( b = -([[p]]+[[q]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the negative sign required by matching \( -(p+q) \) to \( b \).
This is the product of the roots, which is \( c \), not \( b \).
This has the wrong sign on the product of the roots.
This subtracts the roots instead of adding them.
This uses only one root instead of the sum of both.
This is one more than the correct coefficient.
This is one less than the correct coefficient.

stem: The general solution of a second-order constant-coefficient homogeneous equation is \( y(t) = (C_1 + C_2 t)e^{[[r]]t} \), indicating a repeated characteristic root. Writing the equation as \( y'' + b\,y' + c\,y = 0 \), what is the value of the coefficient \( b \)?
solution: A repeated root \( r = [[r]] \) means the characteristic equation is \( (s - [[r]])^{2} = s^{2} - 2([[r]])s + [[r]]^{2} = 0 \). Matching \( y'' + b\,y' + c\,y = 0 \) gives \( b = -2([[r]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the negative sign required to match the repeated-root coefficient.
This uses the root itself instead of twice the root.
This uses the negative of the root instead of twice the negative root.
This confuses \( b \) with the constant term \( c = r^{2} \).
This has the wrong sign on \( c \) as well as reporting the wrong coefficient.
This is one more than the correct coefficient.
This is one less than the correct coefficient.

stem: The general solution of a second-order constant-coefficient homogeneous equation is \( y(t) = e^{[[alpha]]t}\left(C_1\cos([[omega]]t) + C_2\sin([[omega]]t)\right) \), indicating complex conjugate characteristic roots \( [[alpha]] \pm [[omega]]i \). Writing the equation as \( y'' + b\,y' + c\,y = 0 \), what is the value of the coefficient \( c \)?
solution: Complex conjugate roots \( [[alpha]] \pm [[omega]]i \) come from the factorization \( (r - ([[alpha]]+[[omega]]i))(r-([[alpha]]-[[omega]]i)) = r^{2} - 2([[alpha]])r + ([[alpha]]^{2}+[[omega]]^{2}) = 0 \). Matching \( y'' + b\,y' + c\,y = 0 \) gives \( c = [[alpha]]^{2} + [[omega]]^{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This subtracts \( \omega^{2} \) instead of adding it, as if the roots were real.
This gives \( b = -2\alpha \), the coefficient of \( y' \), not \( c \).
This has the wrong sign on \( b \) as well as reporting the wrong coefficient.
This adds \( \alpha \) and \( \omega \) directly instead of summing their squares.
This omits the contribution of \( \alpha^{2} \) entirely.
This is one more than the correct coefficient.
This is one less than the correct coefficient.

stem: The general solution of a first-order linear equation is \( y(x) = Ce^{[[k]]x} + [[s]] \), where \( C \) is an arbitrary constant. Writing the equation as \( y' + a\,y = g(x) \), what is the value of the coefficient \( a \)?
solution: The homogeneous part \( Ce^{[[k]]x} \) solves \( y' + a y = 0 \), i.e. \( y' = -ay \), so \( -a = [[k]] \) and \( a = -[[k]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the negative sign relating the exponent to \( a \).
This inverts the coefficient instead of negating it.
This inverts and negates the coefficient.
This confuses \( a \) with the steady-state value \( s \).
This confuses \( a \) with the negative of the steady-state value.
This is one more than the correct coefficient.
This is one less than the correct coefficient.
